did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs), specifically VGG16, MobileNet, and Inception_resnet_v2, along with transfer learning and soft-attention mechanisms, to classify skin lesions and detect skin cancer using the ISIC and HAM10000 datasets.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents an original intelligent skin cancer detection system using Convolutional Neural Networks (VGG16, MobileNet, Inception_resnet_v2) on ISIC and HAM10000 datasets. It details specific methods (transfer learning, soft-attention, data augmentation), implementation details (Adam optimizer, batch normalization), and reports experimental results (accuracy scores of 0.88, 0.90, 0.93). The structure includes Abstract, Introduction, Method, Results, and Discussion sections typical of original research.

title: Intelligent Skin Cancer Detection System Based on Convolutional Neural Networks
brief_summary: This study proposes an intelligent skin cancer detection system utilizing Convolutional Neural Networks (VGG16, MobileNet, and Inception_resnet_v2) to classify malignant versus benign moles and seven classes of skin lesions. The system integrates a Skin Image Analysis Module, a Medical Chatbot Module for user consultation, and a UI Web Module for image upload and result display. The models were trained and evaluated using the ISIC dataset for binary classification and the HAM10000 dataset for multi-class classification, employing transfer learning and soft-attention mechanisms. The experimental results demonstrated accuracy scores of 0.88 for VGG16, 0.90 for MobileNet, and 0.93 for Inception_resnet_v2. Additionally, the medical chatbot module achieved an intent matching accuracy of approximately 97% during the training phase. The system aims to provide fast, accurate, and diversified feedback to improve early diagnosis and patient survival rates.

lesion_types_studied.details: Used in binary classification task (malign vs benign) using the ISIC dataset. The paper notes that malign moles are mostly melanoma and that the ISIC dataset is biased towards melanocytic lesions (nevi or melanomas).

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of malignant vs. benign moles and multi-class classification of 7 skin lesion types.
all_task_types.details: The paper performs two classification tasks: 1) Binary classification of malignant moles (mostly melanoma) versus benign moles using the ISIC dataset with a VGG16-based model (precision 0.88). 2) Multi-class classification of 7 skin lesion categories (Melanoma, Melanocytic Nevi, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Actinic Keratosis, Benign Keratosis, Dermatofibroma, Vascular lesions) using the HAM10000 dataset with MobileNet and Inception_resnet_v2 (Soft-Attention) models (accuracies 0.90 and 0.93 respectively).

datasets_used.name: ISIC (International Skin Imaging Collaboration: Melanoma Project)
datasets_used.description: A dataset from the ISIC archive, an academia and industry partnership for digital skin imaging. The paper notes it is biased towards melanocytic lesions (nevi or melanomas). It was used for binary classification of malignant vs. benign moles.

use.training: Used to train the VGG16-based model for binary classification of malignant vs. benign moles. The paper states 1208 benign and 849 malignant images were included in the specific subset used for this task.
use.validation: Used for validation (best validation accuracy was saved during training for 100 epochs).

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A publicly available dataset of dermatoscopic images released by the ISIC archive to solve the problem of small size and lack of diversity in available datasets. It contains 10,015 images of size 450x600.

use.training: Used to train the MobileNet-based model and the Soft-Attention Model (Inception_resnet_v2) for multi-class classification of 7 skin lesion types. Images were resized to 299x299 for Inception ResNet v2.
use.validation: Used for validation (validation accuracy reported as 0.74 for MobileNet; early stopping used for Soft-Attention model).
use.testing: Used for testing/evaluation (accuracy reported as 0.90 for MobileNet and 0.93 for Soft-Attention model).
use.other: Repeated images were identified and carefully split to ensure no duplicated images were in the test set.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, F1 score, and confusion matrices without statistical significance testing.

ISIC.task: Binary classification of malignant vs. benign moles

VGG16.notes: Precision shows a high true prediction rate of all the malign skin images. The classification report was generated on a test set of 100 images (50 malignant, 50 benign).

ISIC.notes: The dataset consisted of 13,786 dermatoscopic images, biased towards melanocytic lesions. The specific subset used for this task included 1,208 benign and 849 malignant images.

HAM10000.task: Multi-class classification of 7 skin lesion types (MEL, NV, BCC, AKIEC, BKL, DF, VASC)

MobileNet.notes: All precisions were more than 0.5. High accuracies were observed in predicting Basal Cell Carcinoma, Melanocytic Nevi, and Vascular skin lesions. The model showed better ability in solving multi-class classification problems including non-melanocytic pigmented lesions.

Soft-Attention Model (Inception_resnet_v.notes: Accuracy of every category was more than 0.5. The model got good results in predicting NV, BCC, and VASC. It shows better overall accuracy in multi-classification problems due to the soft-attention unit eliminating irrelevant factors. Although it did not show the same high accuracy in predicting melanoma as MobileNet, its ability to detect other skin diseases was better.

HAM10000.notes: The dataset consisted of 10,015 dermatoscopic images. Repeated images were identified and carefully split to ensure no duplicated images were in the test set. Images were resized to 299x299 for Inception ResNet v2.

architecture_used.main_model: Three CNN models: VGG16, MobileNet, and Inception_resnet_v2 (with Soft-Attention).
architecture_used.pretraining: Transfer learning was used for all three models. VGG16 and MobileNet used pre-trained networks. MobileNet had 4,253,864 pre-trained parameters.
architecture_used.input_size: ISIC dataset images resized to 128x128x3. HAM10000 dataset images resized to 299x299 for Inception ResNet v2. MobileNet input size not explicitly stated but derived from HAM10000 (450x600) or resized for training.

VGG16.additional_layers: One flatten layer and two dense layers added at the end.
VGG16.activation_functions: ReLU for all layers except the last one which uses Sigmoid.

MobileNet.modifications: Last 5 layers removed, one new dense layer added for 7 categories. All layers except the last 23 were frozen.

Inception_resnet_v2.modifications: Soft Attention Block added after the Inception Resnet C block.
Inception_resnet_v2.soft_attention_details: Feature tensor extracted, input to 3D convolution layer, normalized using softmax, aggregated to produce attention map alpha, scaled by learnable scalar gamma, concatenated with original feature. Followed by maxpool layer (2x2), concatenated with another maxpool layer output, followed by ReLU activation and 0.5 dropout layer.

training_details.optimizer: Adam optimizer (learning rate 0.01) used for Soft-attention Model. Not explicitly stated for VGG16 and MobileNet but implied as standard practice or not reported.
training_details.batch_normalization: Batch normalization layer added after each layer in all networks.
training_details.dropout: 0.5 dropout used in Soft Attention unit. Dropout rate 0.5 used in Medical Chatbot module (not skin cancer detection models).

epochs.Soft-Attention (Inception_resnet_v2): Early stopping with patience 30, stopped at epoch 58.

training_details.data_augmentation: Rotations, noising, and scaling used for ISIC dataset. Data augmentation used for HAM10000 to avoid class imbalances.
training_details.normalization: Pixels divided by 255 for Inception ResNet v2.
training_details.initialization: Learnable scalar gamma initialized from 0.01 in Soft Attention unit.

classification_or_output_head.MobileNet: One new dense layer for 7 categories (activation not explicitly stated, likely softmax given multi-class context).
classification_or_output_head.Inception_resnet_v2: Output layer with 7 hidden units followed by softmax activation unit.

preprocessing_techniques: Resizing images to 128x128x3 for the ISIC dataset (VGG16 model)
Resizing images to 299x299 for the HAM10000 dataset (Inception ResNet v2 model)
Data augmentation including rotations, noising, and scaling for the ISIC dataset
Data augmentation to address class imbalances for the HAM10000 dataset
Normalization by dividing each pixel value by 255 to keep values in the range 0 to 1
Identification and careful splitting of repeated images to ensure no duplicates in the test set

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied to the ISIC dataset to address class imbalance between benign and malignant images and to avoid overfitting.
Applied to the ISIC dataset to address class imbalance between benign and malignant images and to avoid overfitting.
Applied to the ISIC dataset to address class imbalance between benign and malignant images and to avoid overfitting.
Used for the HAM10000 dataset to reduce the impact of data imbalance and avoid class imbalances.

conclusions: The proposed intelligent skin cancer detection system, integrating VGG16, MobileNet, and Inception_resnet_v2 with transfer learning and soft-attention mechanisms, achieved accuracies of 0.88, 0.90, and 0.93 on the ISIC and HAM10000 datasets, respectively.

The Soft-Attention Model (Inception_resnet_v2) demonstrated superior overall accuracy (0.93) in multi-class classification of seven skin lesion types compared to MobileNet (0.90) and VGG16 (0.88), attributed to its ability to eliminate irrelevant factors in images.
The VGG16-based model achieved a precision of 0.88 in binary classification of malignant versus benign moles, showing a high true prediction rate for malignant skin images.

The MobileNet model showed high accuracy in predicting Basal Cell Carcinoma, Melanocytic Nevi, and Vascular skin lesions, demonstrating better capability in solving multi-class classification problems including non-melanocytic pigmented lesions.

The Soft-Attention Model achieved good results in predicting Melanocytic Nevi (NV), Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC), and Vascular lesions (VASC), although it did not show the same high accuracy in predicting melanoma as MobileNet.
The integrated Medical Chatbot Module achieved an intent matching accuracy of approximately 97% during the training phase, providing users with timely and reliable consultation feedback.

The system provides a platform for fast, accurate, and diversified skin cancer detection and feedback, aiming to improve early diagnosis and patient survival rates by allowing users to upload images and receive immediate analysis.
important_remarks: The ISIC dataset used for binary classification is biased towards melanocytic lesions (12,893 of 13,786 images are nevi or melanomas), limiting generalizability to non-melanocytic lesions.
The VGG16 model was evaluated on a very small test set of only 100 images (50 malignant, 50 benign), which may not provide a robust estimate of performance.
The MobileNet model showed a significant gap between training accuracy (0.90) and validation accuracy (0.74), indicating potential overfitting.
The Soft-Attention model (Inception_resnet_v2) did not achieve the same high accuracy in predicting melanoma as the MobileNet model, despite having higher overall multi-class accuracy (0.93 vs 0.90).
The study lacks external validation; results are reported only on the ISIC and HAM10000 datasets without testing on independent clinical cohorts.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed to assess the significance of the differences between model performances.
The Medical Chatbot module's intent matching accuracy (97%) was measured only during the training phase, and its performance on unseen user queries is not reported.
The paper does not report sensitivity, specificity, or AUC metrics, relying primarily on accuracy and precision, which may mask performance issues in imbalanced classes.
